title: Sampling
simple_html:
You usually cannot study everyone, so you study a sample — a smaller group chosen to stand in for the whole population. Good sampling lets you draw trustworthy conclusions about the population without surveying every single person.

Two families of sampling

  • Probability sampling — everyone in the population has a known chance of being picked, usually through randomness. This lets you generalise to the population with statistical confidence. Used mostly in quantitative studies.
  • Non-probability sampling — cases are chosen for convenience, judgement, or specific traits. You cannot generalise statistically, but it is fine for exploratory or qualitative work.

Common methods

  • Simple random — pick names out of a hat (probability).
  • Stratified — split the population into groups, then sample each (probability).
  • Cluster — sample whole groups, e.g. entire branches (probability).
  • Convenience — whoever is easiest to reach (non-probability).
  • Purposive — deliberately pick information-rich cases (non-probability).
  • Snowball — participants refer others (non-probability).

Key takeaways

  • A sample stands in for the whole population.
  • Probability sampling allows statistical generalisation; non-probability does not.
  • Bigger, well-chosen samples reduce sampling error.
  • Match the sampling method to your design: probability for surveys, purposive for qualitative work.
intermediate_html:
Sampling is the process of selecting a subset of cases from a target population so that conclusions drawn from the subset can, ideally, be extended to the whole. Because studying an entire population (a census) is usually impractical, sampling is where representativeness, cost, and feasibility are balanced. The first decision is between probability and non-probability sampling; the second is sample size.

Key terms

  • Population: the full set of cases you care about.
  • Sampling frame: the actual list from which you draw (e.g., a customer database). Gaps between frame and population create coverage error.
  • Sample: the cases you actually study.
  • Sampling error: the difference between a sample statistic and the true population value, arising purely from sampling. It shrinks as sample size grows.

Probability sampling

MethodHow it worksWhen to use
Simple randomEvery case has an equal chanceHomogeneous population, good frame
SystematicEvery nth case from a random startOrdered list, no hidden cycles
StratifiedSplit into strata, sample each proportionallyEnsure key subgroups are represented
ClusterSample whole groups, then all/some withinGeographically dispersed populations

Non-probability sampling

MethodHow it worksWhen to use
ConvenienceEasiest-to-reach casesPilots, low-stakes exploration
PurposiveDeliberately information-rich casesQualitative depth studies
QuotaFill preset category targets non-randomlyMarket research, speed matters
SnowballParticipants refer othersHard-to-reach/hidden populations

Worked example

A university with 20,000 students wants to estimate average satisfaction. A stratified random sample is ideal: divide students by faculty (the strata), then randomly sample within each in proportion to its size, so engineering and arts are both fairly represented. To size the sample, the researcher uses a formula targeting a 95% confidence level and a ±5% margin of error, which (for a large population) yields roughly 377 respondents; she over-samples to ~450 to allow for non-response. By contrast, a colleague doing a qualitative study of why some students consider dropping out uses purposive sampling — deliberately recruiting 15 students who have considered leaving — and stops when new interviews stop yielding new themes (data saturation).

How to apply it

  1. Define the population and sampling frame precisely; check for coverage gaps.
  2. Choose the family: probability if you need statistical generalisation (surveys, experiments); non-probability if you need depth or face access constraints (qualitative).
  3. Pick the specific method that fits your design and resources.
  4. Determine size: for quantitative work use confidence level, margin of error, and population size (or a power analysis); for qualitative work aim for saturation.
  5. Anticipate non-response and plan to over-recruit.

Sampling executes the design from Lesson 4 and feeds directly into measurement (Lesson 6) and the inferential statistics of Lesson 8 — whose generalisations are only valid if the sampling was sound.
full_html:
Sampling is the selection of a subset of a population for study, undertaken because complete enumeration (a census) is usually too costly, slow, or impossible. The quality of a study's external validity — its capacity to generalise — rests heavily on the sampling decision, making this one of the most consequential of the inner onion layers (Saunders, Lewis & Thornhill, 2019). The central question is whether each member of the population has a known, non-zero probability of selection, which divides all sampling into two families with very different inferential powers.

Foundational concepts

The target population is the complete set of cases about which one wishes to draw conclusions. The sampling frame is the operational list from which cases are actually drawn; discrepancies between frame and population produce coverage error (e.g., a telephone frame omits households without phones). The sampling unit and the unit of analysis must be clearly defined and may differ. Sampling error — the inevitable discrepancy between a sample statistic and the true parameter due to chance selection — is governed by the standard error, which falls with the square root of sample size (so quadrupling the sample only halves the error). Crucially, sampling error is distinct from, and often smaller than, non-sampling errors such as non-response bias, measurement error, and coverage error, which larger samples do not fix (Groves et al., 2009).

Probability sampling

In probability designs every case has a known chance of selection, enabling statistical inference and quantifiable confidence. Simple random sampling gives each case an equal probability and is the theoretical benchmark. Systematic sampling selects every k-th element from a random start — efficient, but dangerous if the list has a hidden periodicity matching k. Stratified random sampling partitions the population into homogeneous strata (e.g., by region or job grade) and samples within each; it reduces sampling error relative to simple random sampling and guarantees representation of small but important subgroups. Cluster sampling samples naturally occurring groups (e.g., schools, branches) and is economical for dispersed populations, though it typically inflates sampling error (a higher design effect) because cases within a cluster are correlated. Multistage sampling combines these in sequence for large national surveys.

Non-probability sampling

Where a frame is unavailable, generalisation is not the goal, or the philosophy is interpretivist, non-probability designs are appropriate. Convenience sampling takes the most accessible cases — cheap but prone to bias. Purposive (judgemental) sampling deliberately selects information-rich cases and is the workhorse of qualitative research; Patton (2015) catalogues numerous variants (maximum-variation, typical-case, extreme-case, critical-case). Quota sampling fills preset category targets non-randomly and is common in market research. Snowball sampling uses referrals to reach hidden or hard-to-access populations (e.g., undocumented workers), at the cost of network-driven bias. The trade-off is explicit: non-probability designs sacrifice statistical generalisability for feasibility, access, or analytical depth, generalising to theory rather than to a population.

Determining sample size

For quantitative studies, size is driven by the desired confidence level, the acceptable margin of error, the population variability, and population size; for proportions in a large population, a 95% confidence level with a ±5% margin requires roughly n = 384. More rigorously, a power analysis (Cohen, 1988) sets n from the desired statistical power (commonly 0.80), the chosen significance level (α = 0.05), and the expected effect size — ensuring the study can detect an effect of practical importance if one exists. For qualitative studies the logic is entirely different: sample size is governed by data saturation — the point at which additional cases yield no new themes (Guest, Bunce & Johnson, 2006, found saturation often occurs within 12 interviews for homogeneous groups) — not by representativeness.

Worked example

A bank with 600,000 customers wants to estimate the proportion satisfied with a new app, to within ±3% at 95% confidence. Using the proportion formula with p = 0.5 (the most conservative variance), n ≈ 1,067; because the population is large, the finite-population correction barely changes this. Anticipating a 40% response rate, the bank must invite roughly 2,700 customers. It chooses stratified random sampling across age bands to ensure older, less app-active customers are represented. Separately, to understand why dissatisfied customers feel as they do, an analyst uses purposive sampling to recruit 14 low-scoring respondents for interviews, continuing until saturation. The two strands — probability for the estimate, purposive for the explanation — embody a mixed-methods design.

Nuances, debates, and misconceptions

  • Bigger is not always better. A large but biased sample (the classic 1936 Literary Digest poll, which surveyed 2.4 million people yet wrongly predicted the US election due to frame and non-response bias) is worse than a small representative one. Sample quality dominates sample quantity.
  • Statistical vs. analytical generalisation. Probability samples generalise to populations; qualitative and case-study samples generalise to theory (Yin, 2018). Criticising a purposive sample for being "unrepresentative" misunderstands its purpose.
  • Saturation is contested. Critics argue it is invoked vaguely; best practice pre-specifies how saturation will be judged.
  • Non-response bias is frequently the largest threat in surveys and is unaffected by sample size; weighting and follow-ups are partial remedies.
  • Self-selection in online panels and convenience samples systematically distorts results yet is often unreported.

Cross-links

Sampling operationalises the Research Design chosen in Lesson 4 and is constrained by the strategy from Lesson 3 (experiments and surveys demand probability logic; case studies and grounded theory use purposive logic). It is the precondition for valid Inferential analysis in Lesson 8, since confidence intervals and hypothesis tests assume probability sampling, and it interacts with measurement quality in Lesson 6 and with the ethics of participant selection and consent in Lesson 7.
